danger_signals.visual_form: contrasting colored bullets/energy patterns moving toward player
hostile aircraft and mechanical units filling screen space
playfield limits where player ship cannot traverse
large clusters of sprites converging on player position

safety_signals.meaning: safe navigation corridors and breathing room

safety_signals.visual_form: distinctive pickup objects with contrasting colors
safety_signals.meaning: player capability enhancement and resource gain

faction_indicators.color_identity: typically bright/primary colors for player ship sprite
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: player positioned at screen bottom, forward-facing orientation

faction_indicators.color_identity: contrasting darker or secondary palette for hostile units
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: mechanical/military aircraft and weapon platform designs
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: multiple enemy types with distinct silhouettes and attack patterns
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: enemies spawn from top/sides, descend toward player in formation patterns

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: screen filled with enemy sprites, projectile patterns, and visual effects
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemies continuously fire and maneuver; player must dodge and counterattack

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: progressive increase in enemy sprite density and projectile complexity per wave/stage
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemy formations become tighter and more aggressive; attack patterns accelerate

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: stage completion markers; visual transitions between level sets
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemy waves clear; screen resets for next stage with increased difficulty parameters

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: potential visual feedback on player sprite or screen effects indicating hit registration
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player loses health/lives; game-over state triggered if depleted

environmental_storytelling.example: military aircraft and weapon platforms communicate hostile faction identity and combat context through visual design alone
player positioned at bottom suggests defensive posture against invasion from above; top-to-bottom enemy flow implies overwhelming superior force
increasing enemy density and complexity per stage visually communicates intensifying military conflict without dialogue

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: responsive to input; fires projectiles in forward direction; occupies bottom-center screen position
potential visual damage states or flashing indicating vulnerability; reduced maneuverability or firing rate possible
moves in formation patterns; fires projectiles at player; maintains altitude advantage
removed from screen; possible explosion/destruction animation
drops from destroyed enemies or screen edges; moves toward player or drifts passively

notes: Truxton is a vertically-scrolling shoot-em-up with military conflict framing. Visual communication relies entirely on sprite design, spatial positioning, and behavioral patterns rather than narrative text. The game world-state is conveyed through enemy density escalation, projectile pattern complexity, and stage progression. No dialogue or explicit faction lore; conflict identity emerges purely from visual military aesthetic (aircraft, weapons platforms). T2 tier assessment due to lack of detailed source documentation on specific visual design choices.
sources_used: Wikipedia disambiguation entry (confirms game as 1988 arcade release, known as Tatsujin in Japan)
